_meta.authoringNotes: Prevalence figures from WHO global elder abuse meta-analysis (Yon et al. Lancet Glob Health 2017, PMID 28104184) and the US National Elder Mistreatment Study (Acierno et al. Am J Public Health 2010, PMID 20019303). Mortality association from Lachs et al. JAMA 1998 (PMID 9728754). Clinical assessment per the American Geriatrics Society (AGS) position statement 2017 and the USPSTF 2018 update on intimate-partner and elder abuse screening (PMID 30326502).

h1Title: Elder Abuse Syndrome: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Elder abuse syndrome is a clinical pattern of intentional or negligent acts by a caregiver, family member, or trusted other that causes physical injury, emotional harm, financial loss, sexual violation, or unmet basic needs in an adult aged 60 or older. The WHO 2017 meta-analysis estimates roughly 15.7% of older adults living in the community experience at least one form of abuse each year (1 in 6 worldwide), and rates rose substantially during the COVID-19 pandemic. Presentations include unexplained bruises in atypical locations, weight loss, dehydration, pressure injuries, repeated medication errors, sudden bank withdrawals, and social withdrawal. Older adults who experience abuse have approximately three times the 13-year mortality of non-abused peers (Lachs JAMA 1998), even after controlling for comorbidity. Management combines protective intervention through Adult Protective Services, medical and psychiatric care, legal and financial safeguarding, and structured caregiver-support programs.

keyStats.prevalence: ~15.7% of community-dwelling adults aged 60+ globally report abuse in the past year (Yon et al. Lancet Glob Health 2017); 10% in the US National Elder Mistreatment Study
keyStats.demographics: Women report higher rates than men in most studies; rates rise with cognitive impairment and functional dependence; perpetrators are family members (often adult children or spouses) in 60-90% of cases
keyStats.avgAge: Most studies define elder abuse as age ≥60 (US) or ≥65 (some EU and Asian guidelines); incidence rises sharply after age 75
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 141 million older adults experience some form of mistreatment annually worldwide (WHO 2024)

definition: Elder abuse syndrome (ICD-10: T74.91 for confirmed adult abuse, T76.91 for suspected; Y07.x for perpetrator coding) is the WHO-defined category of intentional acts or failures to act, by a person in a relationship of trust, that result in harm or distress to an older adult. The five recognized subtypes are physical abuse, psychological or emotional abuse, sexual abuse, financial or material exploitation, and neglect (including self-neglect when intervention capacity is lost through cognitive decline). Identification rests on a combination of history (often from caregivers and the patient separately), physical findings inconsistent with the explanation, functional decline disproportionate to chronic disease, and contextual red flags such as caregiver dominance during the visit. The American Geriatrics Society, US Preventive Services Task Force, and World Health Organization all classify elder abuse as a public-health priority requiring systematic clinician inquiry, validated screening instruments (Elder Abuse Suspicion Index, Elder Assessment Instrument), and mandatory or strongly encouraged reporting to Adult Protective Services depending on jurisdiction. Geriatricians, primary-care physicians, emergency-medicine clinicians, social workers, and forensic nurses share responsibility for diagnosis. Management is multidisciplinary: medical stabilization of injuries and malnutrition, psychiatric care for depression and PTSD, formal capacity assessment, caregiver-burden interventions, financial and legal protection (powers of attorney, guardianship review, fraud reversal), and safe placement where return to the home setting would put the patient at continued risk.

typesClassification.description: Use of physical force resulting in bodily injury, pain, or functional impairment. Includes hitting, slapping, pushing, restraining, force-feeding, and inappropriate use of medications as chemical restraint. Classic findings are bruises on the inner arms, neck, or genital area, parallel linear marks suggesting restraint, and burns in stocking-glove distribution from forced immersion.

Verbal or non-verbal acts that inflict mental anguish: threats, humiliation, isolation from family and friends, infantilizing speech, and intimidation. Frequently coexists with other forms and produces depression, anxiety, withdrawal, sleep disturbance, and post-traumatic stress symptoms.

Non-consensual sexual contact of any kind, including with adults who cannot consent due to cognitive impairment. Findings may include unexplained genital, anal, or perineal injury, sexually transmitted infections, or bruising of the inner thighs.

Illegal or improper use of an older adult's funds, property, or assets. Includes coerced wire transfers, forged signatures, theft of medications, scam telemarketing, and undue influence over wills. Often the first form detected because bank records create an audit trail.

Failure of a caregiver to meet obligations: untreated wounds, pressure injuries, dehydration, malnutrition, untreated dental and vision problems, expired medications, soiled clothing, and unsafe living conditions. Neglect may be intentional or stem from caregiver burnout, illness, or impairment.

An older adult, often with progressive cognitive impairment, fails to maintain personal hygiene, nutrition, medication adherence, or a safe environment. Accounts for the single largest category of Adult Protective Services referrals in the United States.

primarySymptoms: Unexplained bruises in atypical locations (inner arms, neck, ear, trunk, inner thighs), bruises in different stages of healing, or marks reproducing the shape of a hand, belt, or restraint.

Weight loss greater than 5% over 6 months, sarcopenia, low pre-albumin, and signs of dehydration (orthostatic hypotension, dry mucous membranes, elevated BUN-to-creatinine ratio) without a medical explanation.
Stage 2 to 4 pressure injuries on the sacrum, heels, or ischial tuberosities in a patient with a caregiver who reports turning every two hours.
Sudden behavioral change: withdrawal, fearfulness around a specific caregiver, refusing eye contact, rocking, or new mutism in a previously verbal patient.
Unexplained or repeated injuries presenting late to medical care, with caregiver explanations that change between encounters or are inconsistent with the injury pattern.
Medication non-adherence with empty pill bottles in a patient who is reportedly being managed at home, or sedation out of proportion to prescribed regimens (chemical restraint).
Sudden changes in banking activity, large transfers to a caregiver, new joint accounts, missing valuables, unpaid bills despite adequate income, or a new will favoring a recent acquaintance.
Genital or perineal trauma, new sexually transmitted infection, or bleeding in a cognitively impaired adult.
Poor hygiene, soiled clothing, untrimmed nails, decayed teeth, untreated infestations (scabies, lice), and overgrown wounds in a patient with an identified caregiver.
Repeated emergency-department visits for falls, dehydration, or vague complaints with the same caregiver dominating the history.
earlyWarningSigns: Caregiver answers all questions and will not allow the patient to be interviewed alone
Patient flinches, looks at the caregiver before answering, or appears anxious when specific topics arise
New or escalating verbal conflict observed during the clinical visit
Caregiver expresses resentment, exhaustion, or describes the patient as 'a burden' or 'manipulative'
Bank or care-facility staff report unusual financial activity, unexplained refusal of care, or fear of a specific visitor
emergencySigns: Serious head injury, intracranial bleeding, multiple fractures, or strangulation marks — admit and involve forensic medicine and law enforcement immediately
Sepsis from neglected wounds, pressure injuries, or pyelonephritis in a patient with capacity concerns
Severe dehydration or malnutrition with hemodynamic instability
Suicidal statements or active suicidal plan in a recently identified victim of abuse
Acute sexual assault — preserve forensic evidence, offer post-exposure prophylaxis, and contact a sexual-assault response team within 72 hours

causes.description: Spouses or adult children providing high-intensity care for dementia, stroke, or end-stage organ disease show a 2-3× higher rate of abusive behaviors than the general population. Sleep deprivation, financial stress, social isolation, and absence of respite care are repeatedly identified drivers in cohort studies.

causes.cause: Cognitive impairment in the older adult
causes.description: Dementia is the single strongest patient-level risk factor. Behavioral and psychological symptoms (agitation, aggression, wandering, repetitive questioning) increase caregiver retaliation. In the WHO 2017 meta-analysis, adults with dementia had a roughly 1.4-2× higher abuse prevalence than cognitively intact peers.

causes.cause: Caregiver mental illness or substance use
causes.description: Caregivers with untreated depression, personality disorder, alcohol use disorder, or substance dependence are over-represented among perpetrators. Financial dependence of the perpetrator on the older adult also strongly predicts exploitation and physical abuse.

causes.cause: Social isolation of the older adult
causes.description: Loss of a spouse, friends, and community contacts reduces the chance that abuse is detected and increases dependence on a single caregiver. Isolated older adults are 2-3× more likely to be victims of financial exploitation in US prosecutorial data.

Loss of activities of daily living, mobility, or continence increases hands-on caregiving demand and the vulnerability window for physical and sexual abuse. Each lost activity of daily living raises abuse incidence by roughly 15-25% in longitudinal cohorts.

Understaffed nursing homes, high turnover, inadequate training in dementia care, and weak regulatory oversight increase rates of neglect, chemical and physical restraint, and resident-to-resident aggression. CMS Special Focus Facility designations correlate with higher abuse complaint rates.

riskFactors.factor: Cognitive impairment (Alzheimer's disease, vascular dementia, Lewy-body dementia)
riskFactors.description: Doubles the risk of all forms of abuse; behavioral symptoms specifically predict physical abuse. Early disclosure of dementia diagnosis to clinicians and family enables protective planning.

riskFactors.factor: Functional dependence (≥2 lost activities of daily living)
riskFactors.description: Each lost ADL increases incidence of abuse by 15-25%. Bathing dependence is the strongest individual predictor in several US cohorts.

riskFactors.factor: Living with a caregiver who has untreated mental illness or substance use
riskFactors.description: Strongest perpetrator-level risk factor identified in the National Elder Mistreatment Study and Lachs longitudinal cohort.

riskFactors.factor: Social isolation and absence of confidants
riskFactors.description: Older adults without weekly contact outside the household have 2-3× higher rates of financial exploitation and neglect.

riskFactors.factor: Female sex (some subtypes) and lower socioeconomic status
riskFactors.description: Women report higher psychological and sexual abuse; financial exploitation is more equal across sexes. Lower income and lower education are independent predictors.

riskFactors.factor: Caregiver financial dependence on the older adult
riskFactors.description: Adult children living in the parental home and financially dependent are over-represented as perpetrators of financial exploitation and physical abuse.
Patterns of family violence often persist or re-emerge as the survivor or perpetrator ages, especially when one partner becomes the other's caregiver.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in a long-term-care facility with poor staffing ratios
riskFactors.description: Below-minimum nurse-to-resident staffing and high turnover correlate with documented neglect and pressure-injury rates.

affectedDemographics: Approximately 1 in 6 community-dwelling adults aged 60 and older experience abuse each year worldwide (WHO, Lancet Global Health 2017).
In the US, about 1 in 10 community-dwelling older adults annually; only 1 in 24 cases is reported to Adult Protective Services.
Long-term-care residents have higher rates of psychological abuse and neglect; resident-to-resident aggression affects approximately 20% of nursing-home residents in observational studies.
Older adults with dementia have nearly twice the rate of mistreatment, predominantly by family caregivers.
LGBTQ+ older adults face elevated rates of psychological abuse and isolation, frequently from estranged family members and within long-term-care facilities.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis depends on a structured private interview, careful physical examination, and corroborating findings rather than a single laboratory test. The clinician should interview the older adult alone whenever possible. Validated tools such as the Elder Abuse Suspicion Index (EASI, six items, takes two minutes, sensitivity 47-93%) and the Elder Assessment Instrument (EAI, 41 items for inpatient settings) help structure the conversation and document findings. The physical exam should map and photograph every bruise, abrasion, pressure injury, and burn, noting size, location, color, and shape against a standardized body diagram. Patterned injuries (handprints, ligature marks, bilateral upper-arm bruising) and injuries in unusual locations (back of the ear, palm, soles, axillae, inner thighs) raise specific concern. Laboratory workup includes a complete blood count, basic metabolic panel, albumin, pre-albumin, vitamin D, and a urine drug screen when chemical restraint is suspected. Radiographs are obtained for any deformity, and a low threshold for non-contrast head CT applies to any unexplained head injury. Cognitive assessment with the Montreal Cognitive Assessment or Mini-Cog establishes baseline capacity. The clinician also screens caregivers for burden (Zarit Burden Interview), depression, and substance use. Once mistreatment is suspected, the clinician follows mandatory reporting requirements: in the US, all 50 states have Adult Protective Services pathways and 47 states have mandatory clinician reporting. Documentation must distinguish observed facts from inferences and avoid speculative language to support later civil and criminal proceedings.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Six-question validated screen for suspected abuse in cognitively intact adults aged 65+
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician interviews the patient alone; five questions are asked of the patient and one of the clinician. The screen takes about two minutes and triggers a more detailed evaluation if any answer suggests abuse.

diagnosticTests.purpose: 41-item structured assessment used in emergency departments and hospital wards to document signs of mistreatment
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Completed by a clinician or social worker reviewing history and exam. Each item is scored as definite, possible, probable, or unsuspected. Score guides referral to Adult Protective Services.

diagnosticTests.test: Comprehensive physical examination with body map and photographs
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies and documents bruises, injuries, pressure injuries, hygiene status, and inconsistencies with the offered history
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Examined head to toe by a clinician of the patient's choice of sex when possible. Photographs are taken with consent and a ruler in the frame. Findings are mapped onto a standardized body diagram for the medical record and legal use.

diagnosticTests.test: Cognitive and capacity assessment (Mini-Cog, MoCA, formal capacity evaluation)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes the patient's ability to make medical, residential, and financial decisions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Brief in-office cognitive testing; full neuropsychological assessment if borderline. Capacity is decision-specific and not all-or-none; documentation distinguishes capacity for medical decisions from capacity for financial decisions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies dehydration, malnutrition, infection, fractures, and possible chemical restraint
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Complete blood count, basic metabolic panel, albumin, pre-albumin, urinalysis, urine drug screen, and targeted imaging. Skeletal survey for healing fractures is added when multiple unexplained injuries are present.

diagnosticTests.test: Caregiver assessment (Zarit Burden Interview, PHQ-9, AUDIT-C)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies caregiver burnout, depression, and substance use that increase abuse risk
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Brief self-report questionnaires given to the primary caregiver, ideally separately from the patient. Positive screens trigger respite, treatment, and protective interventions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies exploitation through review of bank statements, beneficiary changes, and powers of attorney
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Conducted in coordination with social work, banking partners, and elder-law attorneys. A formal capacity-to-manage-finances opinion may be requested by courts or banks.

treatmentOverview: Management of suspected elder abuse follows three parallel tracks: medical stabilization, safety intervention, and longitudinal follow-up. Acute medical needs (injury repair, rehydration, wound care, treatment of infection, correction of malnutrition) take priority. The clinician then determines immediate safety: can the patient return to the current setting? If not, hospital admission, an emergency safe placement through Adult Protective Services, or temporary nursing-home placement is arranged. In the US, every state has an Adult Protective Services (APS) agency mandated to investigate; clinicians in 47 states are mandatory reporters. The clinician documents observations precisely and avoids accusatory language; APS makes findings, not the clinician. For cognitively intact patients with capacity who refuse intervention, autonomy is respected while offering safety planning, hotline numbers, and follow-up. Concurrent care addresses depression, post-traumatic stress, and anxiety with structured psychotherapy (cognitive-behavioral therapy, trauma-focused CBT) and pharmacotherapy when indicated. Caregivers receive structured support: dementia-care training (the Savvy Caregiver and REACH II programs reduce abusive behaviors in randomized trials), respite care, treatment of mental illness and substance use disorder, and referrals to caregiver support groups. Financial exploitation triggers urgent bank-level freezes, reversal of fraudulent transfers where possible, revocation of misused powers of attorney, and referral to local prosecutors and the FBI elder-fraud hotline for organized scams. Legal interventions include orders of protection, guardianship review, and assistance with restraining orders. Long-term follow-up at 1, 4, and 12 weeks tracks resolution of injuries, return to function, and recurrence of abuse — repeat mistreatment occurs in 30-40% of identified cases without sustained intervention.

medicalTreatments.name: Adult Protective Services referral and safety planning
medicalTreatments.description: Formal report to the state Adult Protective Services agency with documented observations, contact information, and current safety status. APS investigates, offers services, and coordinates emergency placement if needed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces re-victimization by approximately 30-50% when paired with structured follow-up (US APS outcomes data)

medicalTreatments.name: Wound, fracture, and nutrition management
medicalTreatments.description: Wound debridement, pressure-injury staging and offloading, calorie- and protein-dense feeding (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day protein), correction of electrolytes, vitamin D and B12 repletion, and treatment of dehydration.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pressure injuries heal in 60-80% of patients within 12 weeks when properly offloaded; weight gain of 1-2 kg/month is typical with structured feeding

medicalTreatments.name: Treatment of depression, anxiety, and PTSD
medicalTreatments.description: SSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram) for late-life depression and PTSD; trauma-focused cognitive-behavioral therapy; mirtazapine when weight gain is desirable; avoid benzodiazepines and anticholinergics that worsen cognition.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates 50-65% with SSRI + psychotherapy at 12 weeks in late-life depression trials

medicalTreatments.name: Caregiver support: REACH II, Savvy Caregiver, structured respite
medicalTreatments.description: Evidence-based caregiver education and skills-training programs that reduce caregiver depression, burden, and reported abusive behaviors. Combined with respite care of at least 4 hours per week.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: REACH II randomized trial showed a 30% reduction in caregiver depression and reduced reported mistreatment behaviors at 6 months

medicalTreatments.name: Treatment of caregiver substance use and mental illness
medicalTreatments.description: Referral of the caregiver, with consent, to addiction services, mental-health treatment, and structured family therapy. When the perpetrator refuses care, the focus shifts to separating victim and perpetrator.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Recurrence of abuse falls by 40-60% in cases where caregiver substance use is treated to remission (small US prosecutorial cohort)

medicalTreatments.name: Anti-osteoporotic and fall-prevention therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Calcium 1000-1200 mg/day, vitamin D 800-2000 IU/day, bisphosphonate or denosumab when osteoporosis is documented, physical therapy for strength and balance, and home-hazard removal.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces fracture risk by 30-50% over 3 years; physical-therapy-based fall-prevention programs reduce falls by approximately 20%

surgicalOptions.name: Operative repair of fractures and acute injuries
surgicalOptions.description: Open or closed reduction of hip, wrist, and other fragility fractures sustained during physical abuse or neglect. Standard orthopedic management is supplemented by fall-prevention and bone-health optimization.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 30-day mortality after hip fracture remains 8-10% in older adults; 1-year mortality 20-30%; abuse-related fractures often present late, worsening outcomes

surgicalOptions.name: Wound debridement and reconstruction for advanced pressure injuries
surgicalOptions.description: Stage 3-4 pressure injuries may require surgical debridement, vacuum-assisted closure, and flap reconstruction in selected medically fit patients. Most heal with rigorous conservative care once safe care environment is restored.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reconstruction success approximately 70-80% in selected ambulatory patients; failure rate higher in patients returned to unsafe care settings

alternativeTreatments.name: Restorative care programs and adult day services
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured day programs reduce social isolation, monitor nutrition and hygiene, and give caregivers daytime respite. Associated with reduced caregiver burden and lower nursing-home placement rates in observational studies.

alternativeTreatments.name: Multidisciplinary elder-abuse response teams
alternativeTreatments.description: Hospital and county teams bringing together geriatrics, social work, law enforcement, prosecutors, banking partners, and elder-law attorneys. Improve case identification, prosecution rates, and victim outcomes.

whySeeSpecialist: Geriatricians, geriatric psychiatrists, forensic nurses, and social workers with elder-abuse training identify subtle patterns that general clinicians may miss, perform formal capacity evaluations, coordinate with Adult Protective Services and law enforcement, and design longitudinal safety plans. Specialist involvement increases the rate of successful protective intervention and prosecution.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a geriatrician or geriatric psychiatrist affiliated with a hospital that has an established elder-abuse response team, a forensic nurse program, or a memory clinic. Confirm experience with capacity assessments and willingness to participate in court testimony when needed.
hospitalCriteria: Established elder-abuse response or geriatric-trauma team
Forensic nurse examiner availability for sexual-assault assessment
Social work and Adult Protective Services liaison on staff
Capacity-assessment expertise (geriatric psychiatry or behavioral neurology)
preventionStrategies: Maintain at least one weekly social contact outside the household to reduce isolation, a strong independent risk factor.
Complete advance directives, durable power of attorney for healthcare, and a financial power of attorney while capacity is intact, naming a trusted individual.
Avoid joint bank accounts with non-spouse caregivers; use trust structures and bank fraud alerts on accounts of older adults.
Use respite-care programs and structured day services for caregivers of dementia patients to interrupt cycles of burden-driven mistreatment.
Screen all older adults annually with a validated instrument such as the EASI at primary-care and emergency-department visits.
Train caregivers in dementia behavioral management using evidence-based programs (REACH II, Savvy Caregiver) before crises arise.
lifestyleModifications: Establish a daily check-in routine with a non-caregiver family member, neighbor, faith community, or paid telephone reassurance service.
Keep important documents (will, deeds, financial statements, insurance policies) in a secure off-site location accessible to a trusted advocate.
Maintain mobility and balance through community exercise programs (Tai Chi, Otago) to reduce dependency and fall-related injuries.
Schedule regular medical and dental care to prevent untreated conditions that can be mistaken for neglect.
Review medications quarterly with a pharmacist to identify duplications, anticholinergic burden, and signs of chemical restraint.
Document new injuries with date, time, and photograph when safe and feasible, in a private notebook or smartphone application.

recommended: High-protein foods (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day): eggs, dairy, fish, legumes, poultry
Vitamin D-fortified foods and supplements (800-2000 IU/day)
Fluid intake of 1.5-2 L/day adjusted for cardiac and renal status
Calcium-rich foods (dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens) up to 1200 mg/day
avoid: Long-term excessive alcohol intake, which worsens depression and falls
Restrictive diets without medical indication that may accelerate sarcopenia
Sedating supplements or unverified products marketed for sleep or pain in dementia
Skipping meals, which accelerates weight loss and frailty

exerciseGuidelines: Older adults with capacity should aim for 150 minutes per week of moderate aerobic activity (walking, stationary cycling, swimming) plus twice-weekly resistance training and balance work (Otago, Tai Chi). Caregivers benefit from the same recommendations to reduce their own burden, depression, and risk of burnout-driven abusive behavior.
dailyManagement: Maintain a medication list and pill organizer reviewed weekly with a pharmacist or family member outside the primary caregiver dyad.
Schedule a daily phone or in-person check-in with a non-caregiver contact.
Track weight monthly; report a 2-kg unintended loss to the primary clinician.
Review bank and credit-card activity monthly; set up automatic alerts for transactions above a chosen threshold.
Keep emergency contacts and the Adult Protective Services hotline number posted in an accessible location.
Attend scheduled medical visits even when the caregiver discourages contact with clinicians; arrange transport independently when possible.
prognosis: Outcomes depend on how rapidly and thoroughly the abuse is interrupted. Older adults identified as victims have approximately three times the 13-year mortality of matched non-abused peers in the Lachs JAMA 1998 cohort, with most excess deaths attributable to chronic stress, untreated medical disease, and progression of dementia in unsafe environments. With successful Adult Protective Services intervention, structured caregiver support, and psychiatric care for depression and PTSD, depressive symptoms improve in 50-70% of victims within 6 months and functional decline can be partially reversed. Recurrence is the central prognostic threat: 30-40% of identified victims experience repeat abuse within 1 year without sustained follow-up, falling to under 15% when multidisciplinary teams maintain weekly to monthly contact for the first year. Financial recovery is variable — small amounts can be reversed through bank fraud channels, but large losses, particularly to organized scams or family perpetrators, are rarely recovered. Prosecution rates remain low (under 5% of reported cases lead to criminal charges in most US jurisdictions), but civil remedies (restitution, removal of power of attorney) succeed more often.
recoveryTimeline: Acute injuries (bruises, lacerations, mild dehydration) resolve within 2-4 weeks. Pressure injuries heal over 6-16 weeks with appropriate care. Malnutrition and weight loss reverse over 3-6 months. Depressive and post-traumatic stress symptoms typically improve at 8-12 weeks with combined psychotherapy and SSRI. Long-term safety stabilization and financial restitution often require 6-18 months of multidisciplinary engagement.
complications: Premature mortality with up to threefold higher 13-year death rate compared with non-abused peers — track function and weight quarterly.
Progression of dementia and functional decline driven by stress, malnutrition, and inappropriate medication use — monitor cognition and ADLs every 3-6 months.
Major depressive disorder, generalized anxiety, and PTSD developing in 30-50% of identified victims — screen with PHQ-9 and PCL-5 at follow-up.
Chronic pressure injuries, recurrent UTIs, sepsis, and aspiration pneumonia from sustained neglect — escalate care setting when neglect is unresolved.
Permanent financial loss with risk of homelessness, food insecurity, and inability to pay for needed care.
Repeat victimization in 30-40% of cases without sustained protective follow-up.

supportResources.name: National Center on Elder Abuse (US Administration for Community Living)
supportResources.description: Federal clearinghouse with state-by-state Adult Protective Services hotlines, clinician guides, and training materials.

supportResources.name: Eldercare Locator (US)
supportResources.description: Toll-free 1-800-677-1116 line that connects older adults and families to local Area Agencies on Aging and Adult Protective Services.

supportResources.name: World Health Organization — Elder Abuse Fact Sheet
supportResources.description: Global epidemiology, prevention frameworks, and policy guidance updated 2024.

supportResources.name: AARP Fraud Watch Network
supportResources.description: Free helpline and online resource for older adults and families dealing with scams, identity theft, and financial exploitation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Delirium in Elderly
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Delirium is an acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention and consciousness driven by medical illness, drugs, or infection. It can coexist with abuse, but isolated delirium has identifiable medical triggers and resolves with treatment of the cause. Elder abuse presents with patterned injuries, financial irregularities, and caregiver red flags that persist after acute medical illness is treated.

confusedWithConditions.name: Failure to Thrive in Elderly
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Failure to thrive (weight loss, decreased appetite, physical and cognitive decline) overlaps clinically with neglect but is multifactorial and includes chronic disease, depression, and polypharmacy. Elder abuse should be screened in every failure-to-thrive case; persistence despite optimized medical care, especially with red-flag caregiver behavior, points toward neglect.

confusedWithConditions.name: Alzheimer's Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dementia explains progressive cognitive decline and behavioral disturbances on its own, but it is also the strongest single risk factor for being abused. Clinicians should not stop at the dementia diagnosis; they should still screen for caregiver burden, financial exploitation, and patterned injuries.

confusedWithConditions.name: Depression
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Late-life depression causes withdrawal, weight loss, and reduced self-care. It frequently coexists with abuse and may resolve only when the abusive environment is interrupted. Screen for abuse in every older adult presenting with new depression, especially when a single caregiver dominates the history.

relatedConditions.name: Geriatric Depression
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently develops or worsens after sustained mistreatment; screening and treatment overlap with abuse intervention.

relatedConditions.name: Delirium in Elderly
relatedConditions.relevance: Acute confusion can mask abuse-related injuries or be precipitated by neglect; both demand careful capacity assessment.

relatedConditions.name: Failure to Thrive in Elderly
relatedConditions.relevance: Overlapping syndrome of weight loss and decline; neglect is a leading reversible cause.

relatedConditions.name: Alzheimer's Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Major patient-level risk factor for abuse and a context that complicates capacity and reporting decisions.

relatedConditions.name: PTSD
relatedConditions.relevance: Common psychiatric consequence in survivors of elder abuse, responsive to trauma-focused cognitive-behavioral therapy.

faqs.question: What is elder abuse syndrome in simple terms?
faqs.answer: Elder abuse syndrome is the pattern of harm, neglect, financial exploitation, or sexual mistreatment of an adult aged 60 or older by a person they trust. It includes physical, emotional, sexual, and financial abuse, plus active and passive neglect. The WHO estimates 1 in 6 older adults experience some form of abuse each year.

faqs.question: How common is elder abuse?
faqs.answer: The WHO 2017 global meta-analysis estimates 15.7% of community-dwelling adults aged 60+ experience abuse annually. In the US, about 1 in 10 are affected each year, but only 1 in 24 cases is reported to authorities. Rates are higher in adults with dementia, in long-term-care facilities, and rose during the COVID-19 pandemic.

faqs.question: What are the warning signs of elder abuse?
faqs.answer: Warning signs include unexplained bruises in unusual locations, weight loss, pressure injuries, poor hygiene, sudden withdrawal, fearfulness around a caregiver, missing belongings, unusual bank activity, and a caregiver who answers all questions and will not let the older adult be alone with a clinician.

faqs.question: What are the five types of elder abuse?
faqs.answer: The five recognized types are physical abuse, psychological or emotional abuse, sexual abuse, financial exploitation, and neglect (including self-neglect). Many victims experience more than one type at the same time, most often psychological abuse combined with financial exploitation or neglect.

faqs.question: Who is most likely to abuse an older adult?
faqs.answer: In 60-90% of cases the perpetrator is a family member — most commonly an adult child, spouse, or other relative. Risk rises when the caregiver has untreated mental illness, substance use, or financial dependence on the older adult, and when the older adult has dementia.

faqs.question: How is elder abuse diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines a private interview with the older adult, a structured tool such as the Elder Abuse Suspicion Index, a head-to-toe physical exam with body diagram and photographs, cognitive testing, lab work for nutrition and dehydration, and review of bank and medication records when relevant.

faqs.question: Is elder abuse a crime?
faqs.answer: Yes. In every US state and in most countries, elder abuse, financial exploitation, and serious neglect are crimes and may carry felony penalties. Clinicians in 47 US states are mandatory reporters. Even where prosecution is difficult, civil remedies (restraining orders, guardianship review, recovery of funds) are available.

faqs.question: What should I do if I suspect elder abuse?
faqs.answer: Call your state Adult Protective Services agency or the US Eldercare Locator at 1-800-677-1116. In an emergency, call 911. Clinicians document specific observations rather than conclusions, interview the older adult alone, and arrange immediate safety planning before the patient leaves the encounter.

faqs.question: Does dementia increase the risk of elder abuse?
faqs.answer: Yes. Adults with dementia have approximately 1.4-2 times the rate of abuse compared with cognitively intact older adults. Behavioral symptoms increase caregiver retaliation, and impaired memory makes consistent reporting harder. Caregiver education programs such as REACH II and Savvy Caregiver reduce abusive behaviors.

faqs.question: Can elder abuse cause death?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Lachs JAMA 1998 cohort showed identified victims had approximately three times the 13-year mortality of non-abused peers, even after controlling for chronic disease. Excess deaths come from injury, untreated illness, infection of pressure injuries, malnutrition, dehydration, and progression of dementia in unsafe environments.

faqs.question: What is financial exploitation of older adults?
faqs.answer: Financial exploitation is the illegal or improper use of an older adult's funds, property, or assets. It includes coerced wire transfers, forged signatures, theft of medications, romance and tech-support scams, and undue influence over wills. It is the form most often detected first because banks generate paper trails.

faqs.question: Are nursing-home residents at higher risk of abuse?
faqs.answer: Yes. Long-term-care residents experience higher rates of psychological abuse and neglect than community-dwelling older adults. Resident-to-resident aggression affects about 20% of nursing-home residents. Understaffing, high turnover, and inadequate dementia training are the strongest facility-level predictors.

faqs.question: What is the Elder Abuse Suspicion Index?
faqs.answer: The EASI is a validated six-question screen for suspected abuse in cognitively intact adults aged 65 and older. It takes about two minutes, is administered with the patient alone, and has sensitivity of 47-93% in primary-care studies. Any positive answer prompts a more thorough assessment.

faqs.question: How can families prevent elder abuse?
faqs.answer: Maintain frequent contact, share caregiving responsibilities, use respite-care services for high-burden dementia care, set up financial safeguards (powers of attorney, joint review of bank statements, fraud alerts), and respond early to caregiver burnout or substance use within the family.

faqs.question: Is self-neglect considered elder abuse?
faqs.answer: Yes. Self-neglect — failure to maintain personal hygiene, nutrition, medication, or a safe environment — is the single largest category of Adult Protective Services referrals in the US. It typically reflects cognitive impairment, depression, or social isolation rather than personal choice, and warrants medical and protective evaluation.

faqs.question: Do clinicians have to report elder abuse?
faqs.answer: In 47 US states, healthcare professionals are mandatory reporters of suspected elder abuse. Reporting standards require reasonable suspicion, not certainty. Reports are made to the state Adult Protective Services agency, which investigates and determines findings. Failure to report can result in professional discipline.

faqs.question: Can elder abuse victims recover financially?
faqs.answer: Small fraudulent transfers can sometimes be reversed through bank fraud channels, particularly when reported within 60 days. Large losses to organized scams and family perpetrators are recovered in only a minority of cases, but civil suits, restitution orders, and revocation of misused powers of attorney remain useful.

faqs.question: What role does Adult Protective Services play?
faqs.answer: Adult Protective Services is the state agency mandated to investigate reports of abuse, neglect, and exploitation of vulnerable adults. APS conducts home visits, coordinates emergency placement when needed, connects victims with services, and refers cases to law enforcement and prosecutors where appropriate.

faqs.question: What treatment helps older adults after abuse?
faqs.answer: Treatment is multidisciplinary: medical care for injuries and malnutrition, psychotherapy and SSRI for depression and PTSD, structured caregiver support, financial and legal protection, and longitudinal follow-up at 1, 4, and 12 weeks. Multidisciplinary response teams cut recurrence to under 15% in the first year.

faqs.question: Does elder abuse cause PTSD?
faqs.answer: Yes. Approximately 30-50% of identified elder-abuse victims meet criteria for post-traumatic stress disorder, major depressive disorder, or generalized anxiety. Trauma-focused cognitive-behavioral therapy and SSRIs such as sertraline are effective; benzodiazepines should be avoided in this population.

faqs.question: How does the COVID-19 pandemic affect elder abuse rates?
faqs.answer: Studies during the pandemic showed an increase in self-reported abuse from baseline 10% to 21-24% in some US samples, driven by increased caregiver burden, social isolation, and loss of routine contact with clinicians and community supports. Rates remain elevated above pre-pandemic baselines in several follow-up studies.
